steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: This is a Command of Evidence (Quantitative) question. The underlined claim is that the excitement effect "fades quickly." The correct finding must directly show excitement decreasing over time between announcement and release.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "Consumers tend to prefer new products over older products.": addresses product preference by age, not the fading of excitement after an announcement. ✗
  • "Consumers are more likely to buy a new product when they already use products from the company making the new product.": addresses brand loyalty, not the timeline of excitement after an announcement. ✗
  • "Consumer surveys show low excitement for new products that were announced a long time before they were released.": directly shows that excitement is low when the gap between announcement and release is long — proving the effect fades quickly over that interval. ✓
  • "The kind of information a company provides about a new product helps determine how likely consumers are to buy the product.": addresses the type of information shared, not the rate at which excitement diminishes over time. ✗
That matches the correct answer — C is correct.
